name: Sarvajnatman
dates_notes: Disciple-tradition links to SureŇõvara via DevasvńĀmin/DeveŇõvara; the standard placement is 10th c. with some scholarship extending into the early 11th. Tightened from the older 900‚Äď1100 window.
core_thesis: Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman, claiming descent from SureŇõvara through DevasvńĀmin, builds the proto-*VivaraŠĻáa* Advaita that PrakńĀŇõńĀtman will later systematise. The locus of *avidyńĀ* is Brahman ‚ÄĒ pure *cit* ‚ÄĒ and not the *jńęva* ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 1.319‚Äď321](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.319-321)). The *jńęva* is itself *cidńĀbhńĀsa*, a reflection (*pratibimba*) of Brahman cast in beginningless ignorance ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 2.211‚Äď217; 3.1‚Äď10](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/2.211-217; 3.1-10)). Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman gives this *bimba-pratibimba-vńĀda* its first systematic Advaitic statement, with the standard analogy: one face appears reflected in many mirrors without being multiplied, and so the Brahman-*bimba* appears as plural *jńęva-pratibimbas* without compromising non-duality ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 3.18‚Äď24](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.18-24)). From this root three further commitments follow. The MahńĀvńĀkya is the unique *pramńĀŠĻáa* productive of *akhaŠĻáŠłćńĀkńĀra-vŠĻõtti* ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 1.166‚Äď172](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.166-172), on *tat tvam asi*). *Karma* and *upńĀsanńĀ* remove *vikŠĻ£epa* and *mala* but never produce *j√ĪńĀna* directly; any strong *samuccaya* doctrine is therefore rejected ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 3.336‚Äď345](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.336-345)). Liberation is the *nivŠĻõtti* of avidyńĀ by knowledge alone ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 4.42‚Äď55](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/4.42-55)). PrakńĀŇõńĀtman takes these positions over and refines them in the *Pa√ĪcapńĀdikńĀ-VivaraŠĻáa* (cf. *VivaraŠĻáa* on *Pa√ĪcapńĀdikńĀ* V‚ÄďVI); they form the structural backbone of the post-PadmapńĀda Advaita line.

engaged_works.title: Samkshepa-Shariraka
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored verse-systematization of ŇöaŠĻÖkara's Brahma-SŇętra-BhńĀŠĻ£ya.
engaged_works.summary: Four-*adhyńĀya* metrical *vńĀrttika* in over 2,200 *anuŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ubh* verses, compressing and argumentatively reconstructing ŇöaŠĻÖkara's *Brahma-SŇętra-BhńĀŠĻ£ya*. SureŇõvara's *NaiŠĻ£karmya-Siddhi* and *BŠĻõhadńĀraŠĻáyaka-VńĀrttika* establish the broad principles of the SureŇõvara line; the *SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* welds them into a *Brahma-sŇętra*-shaped system. Four positions are established. First, Brahman is the locus of *avidyńĀ*, explicitly against what will become the *BhńĀmatńę* alternative ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 1.319‚Äď321](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.319-321)). Second, the *jńęva* is *cidńĀbhńĀsa*, analyzed by *bimba-pratibimba* ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 3.18‚Äď24, 3.275‚Äď278](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.18-24, 3.275-278)). Third, the MahńĀvńĀkya is the unique productive *pramńĀŠĻáa* of *akhaŠĻáŠłća-j√ĪńĀna* ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 1.166‚Äď172](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/1.166-172)). Fourth, *karma* has no role in producing knowledge but retains a purificatory function ([*SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka* 3.336‚Äď345](cite://sarvajnatman/samksepa-sariraka/3.336-345)). The work generates a substantial commentarial tradition ‚ÄĒ MadhusŇędana's *SaŠĻÉkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka-SńĀra-SaŠĻÉgraha*, RńĀmatńęrtha's *AnvayńĀrtha-PrakńĀŇõikńĀ*, NŠĻõsiŠĻÉhńĀŇõrama's *Tattvabodhinńę* ‚ÄĒ and supplies the textual base with which later Advaita reckons.

engaged_works.title: Pancha-Prakriya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; ascription is generally accepted in tradition with limited modern critical engagement.
engaged_works.summary: Short pedagogical *prakaraŠĻáa* organizing the central themes of Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman's Advaita under five *prakriyńĀs*, the heuristic procedures by which the student is led from ordinary self-misidentification to recognition of the *akhaŠĻáŠłća* identity. The five-fold rubric typically tracks: the *avasthńĀ-traya-viveka* analyzing waking, dream, and deep sleep to isolate the witness; the *pa√Īca-koŇõa-viveka* of the *Taittirńęya*; the analytic location of *avidyńĀ* in Brahman qua *cit*; the *bimba-pratibimba* exposition of the *jńęva*; and the function of *MahńĀvńĀkya-vicńĀra* in producing the *akhaŠĻáŠłćńĀkńĀra-vŠĻõtti*. The work matters as a compressed handbook of Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman-line *prakriyńĀ* whose categories recur in later VivaraŠĻáa pedagogy. Modern critical engagement remains limited; the ascription is traditional rather than colophon-secured.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
ńĀŇõrayatvaviŠĻ£ayatvabhńĀginńę nirvibhńĀgacitir eva kevalńĀ |
pŇęrvasiddhatamaso hi paŇõcimo nńĀŇõrayo bhavati nńĀpi gocaraŠł• ||

pada_analysis.gloss: of the darkness already established before

samasa_vigrahas.note: The compound compresses the two technical statuses of avidyńĀ: locus and object.

karaka_structure.note: The later-arising conditioned self is the subject of bhavati.
Genitive relation: the darkness/avidyńĀ that is prior to the later entity.

key_passages.english_close: Only undivided consciousness alone possesses the status of locus and object. For what is later-arising does not become, nor can it become, the locus or the object of the darkness that is already established before it.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: AvidyńĀ belongs to partless consciousness itself; the conditioned jńęva or ń™Ňõvara, which arise only after ignorance is operative, cannot bear it as its locus.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: beginningless nescience that veils and projects
the locus in which something resides

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman's cleanest programmatic statement of the Brahman-locus thesis: partless consciousness, not the later conditioned self, bears avidyńĀ as both locus and object. Later VivaraŠĻáa-line arguments repeatedly return to exactly this claim, so the verse fairly represents the line's own self-definition rather than a detachable flourish.
key_passages.source_edition: N. Veezhinathan, The SamkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka of Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman: Critically Edited with Introduction, English Translation, Notes and Indexes, University of Madras, 1972 (archive scan: /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/sarvajnatman_sanksepa_sariraka_veezhinathan_1972_archive.pdf).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
sukŠĻõtaduŠĻ£kŠĻõtakarmaŠĻái kartŠĻõtńĀŠĻÉ
matigatńĀtmacitipratibimbakam |
vrajati tadvad adaŠł• paramńĀtmano
jagati yńĀti tamaŠł•pratibimbakam ||

pada_analysis.gloss: the reflection of self-consciousness that is in the intellect

samasa_vigrahas.note: The compound links consciousness to the mind/intellect as its reflecting medium.

karaka_structure.note: The intellect-reflection is the subject of vrajati.
The sphere in which agenthood is exercised.
The darkness-reflection of the supreme self is the subject of yńĀti.
Genitive relation: the reflection belongs to the supreme self.

key_passages.english_close: The reflection of self-consciousness, the one located in the intellect [buddhi], attains agenthood. It does so with respect to meritorious and demeritorious action. Likewise, that reflection in darkness ‚ÄĒ belonging to the supreme self ‚ÄĒ goes forth as ń™Ňõvara. It thus enters into relation with the world.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Jńęva is the intellect-conditioned reflection that becomes the doer of karma; ń™Ňõvara is the reflection in avidyńĀ that stands in relation to the world-order.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: a reflected image, not an independent second original

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse shows Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman's reflection theory doing explanatory work: the jńęva becomes a karmic agent through intellect-reflection, while the supreme self's darkness-reflection explains worldly lordship. It therefore represents not a decorative metaphor but the operational heart of his bimba-pratibimba model.
key_passages.source_edition: N. Veezhinathan, The SamkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka of Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman: Critically Edited with Introduction, English Translation, Notes and Indexes, University of Madras, 1972 (archive scan: /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/sarvajnatman_sanksepa_sariraka_veezhinathan_1972_archive.pdf).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
upńĀdhim aupńĀdhikam ńĀntaraŠĻÉ cidńĀbhńĀsanaŠĻÉ citpratibimbakaŠĻÉ ca /
cidbimbam evaŠĻÉ caturaŠł• padńĀrthńĀn vivicya jńĀnńęhi tadarthabhńĀjaŠł• //
tathńĀ tvamarthe 'pi catuŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ayaŠĻÉ tad vivecanńęyaŠĻÉ nipuŠĻáena bhŇętvńĀ /
matiŇõ cidńĀbhńĀsanam evam asyńĀŠĻÉ bimbaŠĻÉ tadńęyaŠĻÉ pratibimbakaŠĻÉ ca //

pada_analysis.gloss: referents, entities meant by the word

karaka_structure.note: Objects of jńĀnńęhi in the first half.
The fourfold set in the meaning of tvam is what must be discriminated.
Semantic domain: the meaning of the word tvam.
The consciousness-manifestation occurs in this intellect.

key_passages.english_close: [On the 'tat' side]: there are the upńĀdhi, the inner upńĀdhi-conditioned manifestation of consciousness (cidńĀbhńĀsa), the reflection of consciousness (cit-pratibimba), and the original consciousness-image (cit-bimba). Discriminate and know, thus, these four referents that bear the meaning of 'tat'. Similarly, in the case of the meaning of 'tvam' too, that fourfold set is to be discriminated ‚ÄĒ once one has become exact in analysis. There it is the intellect (mati), the manifestation of consciousness (cidńĀbhńĀsa) in it, the original (bimba) in that, and the reflection (pratibimba) of that original.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: Before one states the identity taught by the mahńĀvńĀkya, one must sort out the four layers involved on each side of the sentence.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: a limiting adjunct that conditions appearance without altering the underlying reality

key_passages.why_this_passage: Rather than extracting only the conclusion of identity, this passage lays out the semantic furniture Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman thinks must be distinguished before interpreting the mahńĀvńĀkya. It is therefore a fair representative passage for his method, because ontology and sentence-meaning are coordinated from the outset instead of being treated as separate topics.
key_passages.source_edition: N. Veezhinathan, The SamkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka of Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman: Critically Edited with Introduction, English Translation, Notes and Indexes, University of Madras, 1972 (archive scan: /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/sarvajnatman_sanksepa_sariraka_veezhinathan_1972_archive.pdf).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
upńĀdhir antaŠł•karaŠĻáaŠĻÉ tvamarthe
jńęvatvam ńĀbhńĀsanam atra tadvat /
tadanvitńĀ cit pratibimbam evam
ananvitńĀŠĻÉ bimbam ńĀhuŠł• ||

karaka_structure.note: The whole verse explains the referents included under the word tvam.
Implicit authorities/teachers are the speaking subject.
What is said to be the prototype.

verb_modality.note: Perfect with present-force reporting an established doctrinal statement.

key_passages.english_close: In the meaning of 'tvam', the limiting adjunct is the internal organ; the manifestation there is jńęva-hood, just as in that other case. Consciousness connected with that is the reflected image; consciousness unconnected with it, they say, is the original image.
key_passages.english_paraphrase: The jńęva is not the original consciousness itself but consciousness as reflected through the internal organ and marked by individual-selfhood.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the internal organ of cognition, especially intellect
the condition of being an individual embodied self

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the clearest compact statement in the available corpus of Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman's account of the tvam-side: the jńęva is consciousness reflected in the internal organ, while the original consciousness remains unassociated. It fairly represents his position because later discussion in the chapter expands this structure rather than replacing it with a different model.
key_passages.source_edition: N. Veezhinathan, The SamkŠĻ£epa-ŇöńĀrńęraka of Sarvaj√ĪńĀtman: Critically Edited with Introduction, English Translation, Notes and Indexes, University of Madras, 1972 (archive scan: /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/sarvajnatman_sanksepa_sariraka_veezhinathan_1972_archive.pdf).
